Including two or more examples in your prompt:

Task: Label customer emails into one of four categories: Billing, Bug, Feature, or Other.

Output JSON:
{
  "category": "<one_of: Billing | Bug | Feature | Other>",
  "reason": "<short explanation>"
}

Examples:

Email: "I was double charged this month and my receipt shows two payments."
→ Category: Billing
Reason: The email is about incorrect charging.

Email: "The app crashes every time I click 'Export to CSV'."
→ Category: Bug
Reason: A specific feature is crashing.

Email: "It would be great if the dashboard could export directly to Google Sheets."
→ Category: Feature
Reason: User is requesting a new capability.

Now label this email:
"Can you add a dark mode option to the settings page?"

pattern with clear examples and output format.

Task:
Extract the following fields from each text: name, email, phone

Examples:
Text: "Hi, I'm John Smith. You can reach me at john@example.com or 555-1234."
JSON: {
  "name": "John Smith",
  "email": "john@example.com",
  "phone": "555-1234"
}

Text: "Contact Sarah Johnson at sarah.j@company.com for more info. Her phone is 555-5678."
JSON: {
  "name": "Sarah Johnson",
  "email": "sarah.j@company.com",
  "phone": "555-5678"
}

Now extract fields from this text:
"Reach out to Mike Brown at mike.b@email.com. Phone: 555-9012"

few-shot prompting significantly improved performance, especially when paired with poorly worded baseline prompts
. It could often compensate for a suboptimal prompt, performing comparably or better than a top zero-shot baseline.
2. Examples are more powerful than many other techniques
— The construct definition, task framing, and (to a lesser extent) examples provided are more influential than additive techniques like persona or chain-of-thought.
— The quality and relevance of the examples you provide directly impacts the model's performance.
that show edge cases and ideal style. More than 5 may consume too much context.
Negative cases (looks like X but is actually Y)

Email: "The app crashes when I click Export."
→ Category: Bug

Email: "Export is too slow."
→ Category: Bug (not Feature, because it's a performance issue)

Email: "I'd love an Export feature."
→ Category: Feature (because it's a new capability request)
to clearly separate examples from instructions:

<examples>
Email: "I was double charged."
→ Category: Billing
Reason: The email is about incorrect charging.

Email: "The app crashes."
→ Category: Bug
Reason: A specific feature is crashing.
</examples>
This is especially effective with Claude, which responds well to
4. The specific examples matter more than the number
The benefit of few-shot prompting is less pronounced when the underlying prompt is already effective, but
the specific examples chosen were critical
. High-quality, relevant examples are more important than just having many examples.
For simple tasks, start with just the instruction and see if the result is good. Add examples selectively when you need to constrain or shape the output.
